columns.extraction_plan: The add-on treatment is Darolutamide. This is explicitly stated in the abstract on page 1: "Patients with metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer were randomly assigned to darolutamide or placebo plus androgen-deprivation therapy and docetaxel." This indicates that darolutamide (or placebo) is added to the standard backbone of ADT and docetaxel. The "Trial Design" section on page 2 further confirms this: "Enrolled patients were randomly assigned (1:1) to oral darolutamide 600 mg twice daily or matched placebo, in combination with ADT and docetaxel."

columns.extraction_plan: This column refers to the overall risk judgment of individual criteria of evidence in randomized controlled trials. This type of assessment (e.g., risk of bias assessment) is typically performed by external reviewers in systematic reviews or meta-analyses, not usually reported within a primary clinical trial publication itself. The ARASENS trial report does not contain an overall risk judgment of its own individual criteria of evidence.

columns.extraction_plan: The control arm is explicitly stated in the abstract and methods section. On page 1, in the "METHODS" section of the abstract, it states: "Patients with metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer were randomly assigned to darolutamide or placebo plus androgen-deprivation therapy and docetaxel." On page 2, in the "Trial Design" section under "METHODS", it states: "Enrolled patients were randomly assigned (1:1) to oral darolutamide 600 mg twice daily or matched placebo, in combination with ADT and docetaxel."

On page 8, refer to Table 2, "Secondary Efficacy End Points for the Subgroups of Patients by Disease Volume and Disease Risk in ARASENS". Locate the column header "All Patients - Placebo (n = 654)". The number of participants in the control arm is 654.

The abstract on page 1 (Chunk 8) states, "Patients with metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer were randomly assigned to darolutamide or placebo plus androgen-deprivation therapy and docetaxel." The Methods section on page 2 (Chunk 8) further clarifies, "Enrolled patients were randomly assigned (1:1) to oral darolutamide 600 mg twice daily or matched placebo, in combination with ADT and docetaxel." The Discussion section on page 6 (Chunk 9) explicitly confirms, "The results of this post hoc analysis of the ARASENS trial, in which all patients received docetaxel, show that darolutamide in combination with ADT and docetaxel increases OS in patients with metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer." Based on these statements, 100% of participants in the Darolutamide + docetaxel + ADT arm received docetaxel. The exact count (N) for this specific arm is not provided in the given chunks.

The abstract on page 1 (Chunk 8) states, "Patients with metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer were randomly assigned to darolutamide or placebo plus androgen-deprivation therapy and docetaxel." The Discussion section on page 6 (Chunk 9) explicitly confirms, "The results of this post hoc analysis of the ARASENS trial, in which all patients received docetaxel, show that darolutamide in combination with ADT and docetaxel increases OS in patients with metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer." Based on these statements, 100% of participants in the Placebo + docetaxel + ADT arm received docetaxel. The exact count (N) for this specific arm is not provided in the given chunks.

The number and percentage of participants with a Performance Status (PS) score of 0 in the Darolutamide treatment arm can be derived by summing the counts from the "High Volume" and "Low Volume" subgroups in Table 1. From Table 1, page 5, under "ECOG PS, No. (%)", for "0": Darolutamide (n = 497) High Volume: 343 (69.0%), Darolutamide (n = 154) Low Volume: 123 (79.9%). Summing these gives: 343 + 123 = 466 patients. The total number of Darolutamide patients in these subgroups is 497 + 154 = 651. The percentage is (466 / 651) * 100 = 71.6%. Therefore, the value is 466 (71.6%).

The number and percentage of participants with a Performance Status (PS) score of 0 in the Placebo control arm can be derived by summing the counts from the "High Volume" and "Low Volume" subgroups in Table 1. From Table 1, page 5, under "ECOG PS, No. (%)", for "0": Placebo (n = 508) High Volume: 349 (68.7%), Placebo (n = 146) Low Volume: 113 (77.4%). Summing these gives: 349 + 113 = 462 patients. The total number of Placebo patients in these subgroups is 508 + 146 = 654. The percentage is (462 / 654) * 100 = 70.6%. Therefore, the value is 462 (70.6%).

Table 1, page 5, reports ECOG PS scores as "0" or "1". No patients are reported with PS 2. Therefore, "PS 1-2" is interpreted as "PS 1". The total number of Darolutamide patients in these subgroups is 497 + 154 = 651. The percentage is (185 / 651) * 100 = 28.4%. Therefore, the value is 185 (28.4%).

The PDF reports on "time to worsening of disease-related physical symptoms as measured by the Functional Assessment of Cancer Therapy-Prostate (National Comprehensive Cancer Network) symptom index 17-item questionnaire" (page 3, text, "Study Assessments" section, and page 8, Table 2). While this is a patient-reported outcome related to quality of life, it specifically measures physical symptoms rather than an overall "Quality of Life Scale" assessing general well-being and satisfaction with health status as per the definition. Therefore, an overall Quality of Life Scale is not reported.

The provided chunks (pages 1-4) do not contain any information indicating that the clinical trial reports results on the quality of life of participants, nor do they contain the specific quotes or table references mentioned in the original plan. Therefore, based on the provided chunks, the answer is No.

columns.column_name: Reporting by prognostic groups - Y/N | Synchronous
columns.extraction_plan: Yes. The text on page 2, in the "METHODS" section, states: "In ARASENS, the treatment effect of darolutamide on OS was favorable in patients with de novo (HR, 0.71; 95% CI, 0.59 to 0.85) and recurrent (HR, 0.61; 95% CI, 0.35 to 1.05) metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer." "De novo" refers to synchronous disease. Additionally, Table 1 on page 5, under "Metastasis stage at initial diagnosis, No. (%)", includes "De novo mHSPC" as a subgroup. Figure A1 on page 16 also presents OS results for the "De novo" subgroup under "Metastatic stage at initial diagnosis".
columns.column_name_raw: Reporting by prognostic groups - Y/N | Synchronous

columns.column_name: Reporting by prognostic groups - Y/N | Metachronous
columns.extraction_plan: Yes. The text on page 2, in the "METHODS" section, states: "In ARASENS, the treatment effect of darolutamide on OS was favorable in patients with de novo (HR, 0.71; 95% CI, 0.59 to 0.85) and recurrent (HR, 0.61; 95% CI, 0.35 to 1.05) metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer." "Recurrent" refers to metachronous disease. Additionally, Table 1 on page 5, under "Metastasis stage at initial diagnosis, No. (%)", includes "Recurrent mHSPC" as a subgroup. Figure A1 on page 16 also presents OS results for the "Recurrent" subgroup under "Metastatic stage at initial diagnosis".
columns.column_name_raw: Reporting by prognostic groups - Y/N | Metachronous

columns.column_name: Reporting by prognostic groups - Y/N | High volume
columns.extraction_plan: Yes. The "PURPOSE" and "RESULTS" sections on page 1 explicitly mention reporting by disease volume. The "METHODS" section on page 2 defines "High-volume disease". Table 1 on page 5 is titled "Baseline Demographics and Patient Characteristics for the Subgroups of Patients by Disease Volume and Disease Risk in ARASENS" and includes a "High Volume" column. Figure 2A on page 6 shows OS for "high-volume disease". Table 2 on page 8, Table 3 on page 9, and Table 4 on page 10 all present data for the "High Volume" subgroup. Figure A1 on page 16 also presents OS results for the "High Volume" subgroup.
columns.column_name_raw: Reporting by prognostic groups - Y/N | High volume

columns.column_name: Reporting by prognostic groups - Y/N | Low volume
columns.extraction_plan: Yes. The "PURPOSE" and "RESULTS" sections on page 1 explicitly mention reporting by disease volume. The "METHODS" section on page 2 defines "Low-volume and low-risk disease". Table 1 on page 5 is titled "Baseline Demographics and Patient Characteristics for the Subgroups of Patients by Disease Volume and Disease Risk in ARASENS" and includes a "Low Volume" column. Figure 2B on page 6 shows OS for "low-volume disease". Table 2 on page 8, Table 3 on page 9, and Table 4 on page 10 all present data for the "Low Volume" subgroup. Figure A1 on page 17 also presents OS results for the "Low Volume" subgroup.
columns.column_name_raw: Reporting by prognostic groups - Y/N | Low volume

columns.extraction_plan: The secondary endpoints are mentioned in the abstract on page 1. These include: Time to castration-resistant prostate cancer, and Time to initiation of subsequent systemic antineoplastic therapy.
The endpoint 'Time To Progression of Serum Androgen (TTPSA)' is not found in the provided chunks.
The endpoint 'Time to Progressive Serum Androgen (TTPSA)' is not found in the provided chunks.

The total number of participants is 1,305. This value can be found in the "RESULTS" section of the abstract on page 1, which states: "Of 1,305 patients, 1,005 (77%) had high-volume disease and 912 (70%) had high-risk disease."

For the Darolutamide + docetaxel + ADT arm, sum the 'n' values from the 'High Volume - Darolutamide (n = 497)' column header and the 'Low Volume - Darolutamide (n = 154)' column header in Table 1. For the Placebo + docetaxel + ADT arm, sum the 'n' values from the 'High Volume - Placebo (n = 508)' column header and the 'Low Volume - Placebo (n = 146)' column header in Table 1.

Extract the regimen for the experimental arm from the "PURPOSE" section of the abstract on page 1: "darolutamide or placebo plus androgen-deprivation therapy and docetaxel." The darolutamide arm is the experimental arm. Further confirmation is found in the "Trial Design" section on page 2: "Enrolled patients were randomly assigned (1:1) to oral darolutamide 600 mg twice daily or matched placebo, in combination with ADT and docetaxel."

The experimental treatment arm is reported in the abstract and methods section. Extract the name of the arm from the "METHODS" section on page 1, which states: "Enrolled patients were randomly assigned (1:1) to oral darolutamide 600 mg twice daily or matched placebo, in combination with ADT and docetaxel." The experimental arm is the one receiving darolutamide.

The treatment regimen consists of three classes of therapy. These are explicitly mentioned in the "INTRODUCTION" section on page 1 (Chunk 8) and the "abstract" on page 1 (Chunk 8). The combination of these three is also referred to as "triplet therapy" in the "Relevance" section on page 2 (Chunk 8).
The trial name "ARASENS" is explicitly stated in the title of the paper and throughout the text.

The type of therapy for the intervention arm is "Darolutamide plus androgen-deprivation therapy (ADT) and docetaxel". This is explicitly stated in the title of the article and in the "METHODS" section of the abstract.

columns.column_name: Volume of disease - N (%) | High | Treatment
columns.extraction_plan: From Table 1, on page 5, locate the column 'High Volume - Darolutamide (n = 497)'. The number of patients is 497. The total number of patients in the Darolutamide arm is the sum of patients in 'High Volume - Darolutamide (n = 497)' and 'Low Volume - Darolutamide (n = 154)', which is 497 + 154 = 651. Calculate the percentage as (497 / 651) * 100.
columns.column_name_raw: Volume of disease - N (%) | High | Treatment

Calculate the percentage as (508 / 654) * 100.
